segments.purgatorio-11-g76: E qui convien introduces an impersonal necessity ('it is necessary here') followed by ch’io + present subjunctive. The clitic ch’ is the elided che ('that') introducing the subordinate clause; io is the subject. Questo peso is the direct object, and porti is the 1st-person present subjunctive of portare (literally 'that I carry/bear'), required by the impersonal convien che. Per lei means 'for her' or 'on her account'.
segments.purgatorio-11-g77: Tanto che here introduces a result/purpose clause ('so that/until'), with a + Dio marking the indirect object ('to God'). Si sodisfaccia is the 3rd-person singular present subjunctive of soddisfare used impersonally with si ('may be satisfied' / 'may it satisfy'), matching the sense of an expressed purpose or termination condition.
segments.purgatorio-11-g78: Poi ch’ introduces a causal clause ('since/for that'), with ch’ = che. Nol is the clitic combination non + lo ('not it' = 'it not'), placed before the verb in the archaic word order. Fe’ is an archaic past form meaning 'I did' (from fare/feci), so poi ch’io nol fe’ = 'since I did not do it'. Tra vivi means 'among the living'.
segments.purgatorio-11-g79: Qui tra ’ morti is a short locative phrase ('here among the dead'). Qui repeats the deictic 'here' for contrast with 'tra vivi' above; tra governs the plural noun morti ('the dead'). The trailing quote closes the spoken line.
segments.purgatorio-11-g80: 'Ascoltando' is a gerund (listening/while listening) giving the circumstance for the main action; 'chinai' is passato remoto, first person singular ('I bowed'/'I bent'); 'in giù' is an adverbial phrase 'downward'; 'la faccia' is the direct object 'the face'. The overall order is gerund phrase + main verb + adverbial + object.
segments.purgatorio-11-g81: 'e un di lor' = 'and one of them'; 'di lor' is the archaic genitive 'of them' (modern 'di loro'). Word order is straightforward: conjunction + indefinite + prepositional phrase identifying membership.
segments.purgatorio-11-g82: This parenthetical clause 'non questi che parlava' means 'not this one who was speaking'; 'non' negates, 'questi' is a demonstrative pronoun ('this one' / 'the present one'), 'che' introduces the relative clause, and 'parlava' is the imperfect indicative (he/that one 'was speaking').
segments.purgatorio-11-g83: 'si torse' is reflexive passato remoto/remote perfect (he twisted/turned himself); 'sotto il peso' is a prepositional phrase 'under the weight/burden'; 'che li 'mpaccia' is a relative clause 'that hinders/presses upon them' — 'li' is the old direct object 'them' and '’mpaccia' is the contracted poetic form of 'impaccia'/'impacciare' (present third person) meaning 'hinders' or 'embarrasses'.
segments.purgatorio-11-g84: Three coordinated verbs describe his reaction: 'videmi' and 'conobbemi' are older-looking past forms with enclitic 'mi' ('he saw me', 'he recognized me'), while 'chiamava' is imperfect ('he called'/'kept calling'). The series of verbs is linked by 'e' (and).
segments.purgatorio-11-g86: A single-word interjection expressing surprise or calling attention; identical to English 'Oh!'.
segments.purgatorio-11-g87: Comma marking the boundary between the exclamation and the narrator's speech; no lexical content.
segments.purgatorio-11-g88: 'diss' io lui' = 'I said to him'; 'diss'' is an archaic past-tense form of 'dire' with the subject 'io' following it and the indirect object 'lui' after, so the clause reports the speaker addressing that person.
segments.purgatorio-11-g89: Rhetorical question 'non se' tu Oderisi' = 'aren't you Oderisi?'; 'non' negates, 'se' is the older second-person singular of 'essere' ('you are'), and 'tu' is the emphatic pronoun.
segments.purgatorio-11-g90: Appositive identifying Oderisi: 'l'onor d'Agobbio e l'onor di quell' arte' = 'the honour/glory of Gubbio and the honour of that art'; 'ch' alluminar chiamata è in Parisi' is a relative clause 'which is called illumination in Paris' with 'chiamata è' functioning like a passive ('is called') and 'alluminar' naming the craft (manuscript illumination).
segments.purgatorio-11-g56: E = 'and'. s’io non fossi impedito is a conditional clause: s’ (se) + io + fossi (imperfect subjunctive of essere) + impedito (past participle) = 'if I were not hindered'. dal sasso = dalla + sasso, here agent ('by the rock'). che introduces a relative clause modifying sasso; la cervice mia superba = 'my proud neck' (adjective after noun, mia agreeing with cervice). doma is a present verb (from domare) 'tames/keeps down', used poetically in place of a more expected tense but functioning as a relative-clause verb.
segments.purgatorio-11-g57: onde = 'wherefore/therefore' (indicates cause/result: 'because of which'). portar is the simple infinitive 'to carry/hold'. convienmi is impersonal convien(e) + enclitic mi = 'it is necessary for me' / 'I must'. il viso basso is the thing one must carry: literal 'the face low' = 'keep my face down'. Word order keeps the impersonal verb before the logical object.
segments.purgatorio-11-g58: cotesti is an archaic demonstrative (plural) meaning 'these' or 'those people'; it stands alone as the direct object the speaker will look at.
segments.purgatorio-11-g59: ch’ancor vive = che ancora vive, a relative clause 'who are still alive' (ancor = ancora). e non si noma is an archaic passive/reflexive construction: non si noma = 'are not named/called' (si = reflexive/passive marker, noma an older form meaning 'named'); together 'who are still alive and are not named/known'.
segments.purgatorio-11-g60: guardere’ io is an older/fixed poetic form meaning 'I would look' (conditional/future-in-the-past effect); the subject pronoun io is postposed for emphasis. The apostrophe marks the omitted vowel typical of Dante's spelling.
segments.purgatorio-11-g61: per veder = 'in order to see' (per + infinitive). s’i’ ’l conosco = se io lo conosco: s’ = se (if/whether), i’ = io (I, here with archaic orthography), ’l = lo (him/it), conosco = present indicative 'I know/recognize' — together 'to see whether I recognize him'.
segments.purgatorio-11-g62: e per farlo pietoso a questa soma = 'and to make him compassionate/pitying toward this soma.' per farlo = 'in order to make him' (fare + pronoun lo). pietoso = 'pitying/compassionate' modifying the understood agent (him). a questa soma = 'toward this soma' (a indicates the target of the pity); soma is used in the older/poetic sense 'burden/body' here.
segments.purgatorio-11-g63: Io (subject pronoun) + fui (passato remoto of essere) = 'I was'. 'latino' is a predicative adjective/noun meaning 'Latin'. 'e nato' uses the past participle 'nato' (= 'born') in coordinate predication with 'latino'. 'd’un gran Tosco' = 'of a great Tuscan' (di + un, with the epic adjective 'gran' before the noun Tosco). The order is characteristic of poetic diction.
segments.purgatorio-11-g64: Proper name as subject ('Guiglielmo Aldobrandesco') followed by fu (passato remoto of essere) = 'was' and the predicate nominative 'mio padre' ('my father'). Word order is straightforward: Name + foi (was) + mio padre.
segments.purgatorio-11-g65: 'Non so' = 'I do not know' (present indicative of sapere). 'se' introduces an indirect question ('whether/if'); '’l' is the elided article 'il' before 'nome'. Possessive 'suo' follows the noun (typical Italian order). 'già mai fu' uses passato remoto 'fu' with adverbs 'già mai' meaning 'ever/ever before' (an emphatic combination), and 'vosco' is an archaic/regional form meaning 'with you' or 'among you'.
segments.purgatorio-11-g66: The coordinated subject 'L’antico sangue e l’opere leggiadre d’i miei maggior' ('the ancient blood and the graceful deeds of my elders') takes the verb 'fer' (an archaic form of 'fecero' used here) with the indirect object pronoun 'mi' before it: 'mi fer' = 'made me'. 'sì arrogante' is a result phrase ('so arrogant'). Note the poetic compression and archaic forms ('fer' for 'fecero', 'maggior' for 'maggiori').
segments.purgatorio-11-g67: 'che' here introduces the result clause that follows ('that'/'so that'). It links the statement of cause to what resulted from it.
segments.purgatorio-11-g68: 'non pensando' is a gerund/present participle meaning 'not thinking' or 'without thinking'. 'a la comune madre' = 'of/to the common mother' (the preposition a + article la; 'comune madre' = 'common mother'). The whole phrase gives the circumstance under which the result occurred.
segments.purgatorio-11-g69: 'ogn’ uomo' = 'every man' (ogni with elision). 'ebbi in despetto' is an idiom: 'I had (held) in contempt' — ebbi (passato remoto of avere) + noun 'despetto' = 'contempt/spite'. 'tanto avante' is a degree phrase 'so far ahead / to such an extent' that introduces the consequence in the next clause.
segments.purgatorio-11-g70: 'ch’io' = 'that I' (che + io); 'ne' is partitive/causal here ('of it' / 'from that'); 'mori’' is the passato remoto ('mori'' with apostrophe for elision) of morire = 'I died'. Together: 'that I died of it.'
segments.purgatorio-11-g71: 'come' = 'as' introducing a comparison or example. 'i Sanesi' = 'the Sienese (people of Siena)' and 'sanno' is present indicative 'know' — 'as the Sienese know.'
segments.purgatorio-11-g72: 'e sallo' = 'and know it' (sallo is sa + lo, the verb 'sa' with the enclitic object 'lo'); 'in Campagnatico' names the place; 'ogne fante' = 'every lowly man/peasant' (ogne = ogni). The clause adds that everyone in that village knows this.
segments.purgatorio-11-g73: 'Io sono' = present 'I am' (identity statement) + proper name 'Omberto' as predicate nominative. Simple, present-tense copula construction.
segments.purgatorio-11-g74: Poetic/inverted word order here: 'e non pur a me danno superbia fa' = 'and pride does harm not only to me.' 'non pur' = 'not only', 'a me' marks the indirect object, 'danno' functions as the noun 'harm/damage' (placed before the verb for emphasis), 'superbia fa' = 'pride makes/causes'. The verb 'fa' agrees with 'superbia'.
segments.purgatorio-11-g75: 'ché' = 'for/because' introducing the reason. 'tutti miei consorti' = 'all my companions' (direct object). 'ha ella tratti' = literal order 'has she drawn' (ha + past participle tratti = 'has drawn'), with 'seco' = 'with her'. 'nel malanno' = 'into the misfortune' — altogether: 'for she has drawn all my companions with her into misfortune.' Note the auxiliary 'ha' + past participle forms a perfect sense.
segments.purgatorio-11-g1: "O" is the vocative interjection (an address). "Padre nostro" is a direct vocative: "our Father" (word order is the normal noun + possessive for address).
segments.purgatorio-11-g2: "che" is a relative pronoun here meaning "who"; "ne' cieli" is an archaic/in poetic contraction meaning "in the heavens"; "stai" is 2nd-person singular present (from stare) used here as "you dwell/you are" — together forming the relative clause "who dwell in the heavens."
segments.purgatorio-11-g3: "non" is the simple negation "not"; "circunscritto" is a past-participle/adjective meaning "circumscribed, confined, limited." The phrase negates that attribute of the Father: "not confined."
segments.purgatorio-11-g4: "ma" is the adversative "but" introducing a contrast; "per" here means "by/through" (expressing cause or means); "più amore" = "a greater love"; "ch'" is the contracted "che" introducing a comparative clause ("than"); "ai primi effetti di là sù tu hai" = "you have (tu hai) with respect to the first effects up there" — literal: "than (you) have toward the first effects up there." The clause compares the Father's love to the love connected to the 'first effects' above.
segments.purgatorio-11-g5: "laudato sia" is a passive subjunctive/optative formula meaning "may (he) be praised" (literally "praised be"); "'l tuo nome e 'l tuo valore" = "your name and your power/valor" (the elided article 'l = il); "da ogne creatura" = "by every creature." The construction is a wish/ prayer in the passive voice.
segments.purgatorio-11-g6: "com' è degno" = "as/which is fitting" (com' = come); "di render grazie" = "to render/give thanks" where "render" is the infinitive used in the sense "to render/give"; "al tuo dolce vapore" = "to/for your sweet vapore (breath/exhalation)" — an image of the divine out-breath. The clause explains in what manner/for what reason praise is due.
segments.purgatorio-11-g7: "Vegna" is an optative/third-person subjunctive form meaning "may (it) come"; "ver'" is an archaic contraction of verso (here meaning "toward/near") — the full sense is "may the peace of your kingdom come to us." Word order places the verb first (optative) for emphasis.
segments.purgatorio-11-g8: "ché" = "for/because" introduces the reason; "noi ad essa non potem da noi" literally "we to it cannot by ourselves" — "potem" is an archaic form of "possiamo" (to be able), so the clause means "for we cannot get to it by ourselves."
segments.purgatorio-11-g9: "s'ella non vien" is a conditional protasis: "if she/it does not come" (s' = se, if; "ella" = it/she; "vien" = comes). It introduces the condition that would prevent reaching the peace.
segments.purgatorio-11-g10: "con tutto nostro ingegno" = "with all our ingegno (effort/ingenuity)." "ingegno" here means human effort/skill/ingenuity; the phrase qualifies the inability stated before: even with all our wit/energy, we cannot reach it unless it comes.
segments.purgatorio-11-g11: "Come" = "as/just as"; "del suo voler" = "by/according to his/your will" (suo refers to the divine will); "li angeli tuoi" = "your angels" (article + noun + possessive) and "fan" is the archaic present plural of fare (fanno) meaning "make/offer"; "sacrificio a te" = "sacrifice to you." The clause sets up the comparison.
segments.purgatorio-11-g12: "cantando" is a present gerund/participle meaning "singing" or "chanting"; "osanna" is the acclamation "Hosanna" (used as the word the angels utter while offering praise).
segments.purgatorio-11-g13: "così facciano" = "so/thus may (they) do" — "facciano" is 3rd-person plural subjunctive expressing a wish/optative ("may they do"); "li uomini de' suoi" = "the men of his" = "his men/men of his (i.e. mankind under him)." The clause is the wished-for parallel to the angels' action.
segments.purgatorio-11-g14: "Dà" is a verb form from dare (to give); in the prayer context it functions as an address/request to God (imperative/optative sense: "give"); "oggi a noi" = "today to us"; "la cotidiana manna" = "the daily manna" ("cotidiana" = daily).
segments.purgatorio-11-g15: "sanza" = archaic "senza" (without); "la qual" = "which" (referring to the manna); "per questo aspro diserto" = "through/over this harsh desert" (adverbial phrase); "a retro va" = "goes backward" (a + retro = back/behind; va = goes); "chi più di gir s'affanna" = "whoever more (più) to go (di gir) labors/strives (s'affanna)" — an idiomatic relative/indefinite clause meaning "whoever strains most to go on (forward) finds himself going backward." The structure uses an archaic reflexive verb s'affanna and a comparative sense "chi più..." to describe the paradoxical effect.
segments.purgatorio-11-g91: This segment is just a single punctuation mark (a period) standing alone as the entire 'sentence' here; there are no words to parse or explain.
segments.purgatorio-11-g92: A vocative: Frate is the address 'Brother' used to call or address the other person directly. It stands alone and is punctuated as the start of reported speech.
segments.purgatorio-11-g93: diss' elli is the narrative tag 'he said.' diss' is an archaic contracted form of disse (past of dire), here reported as simple past 'said'; elli is a third-person pronoun 'he' used as the subject.
segments.purgatorio-11-g94: This clause is the reported speech's first portion. più is the comparative 'more'; ridon is an archaic 3rd-person-plural present of ridere used metaphorically ('shine/gleam' or literally 'laugh') and takes le carte as its subject ('the pages/papers'). che is a relative pronoun 'that/which'; pennelleggia is a present indicative meaning 'paints with a brush' (from pennelleggiare) and takes Franco Bolognese as its subject/naming the painter.
segments.purgatorio-11-g95: l'onore è tutto or suo is a simple nominal clause: l' onore 'the honor' is the subject; è is the present tense 'is'; tutto 'entirely/all' modifies the subject complement; or is the archaic word for 'now'; suo is the possessive 'his' (referring back to Franco).
segments.purgatorio-11-g96: e mio in parte is a short coordination: e 'and'; mio is the possessive 'my'; in parte is an idiom meaning 'in part' or 'partly,' limiting the extent of the speaker's share of the honor.
segments.purgatorio-11-g97: Ben non sare' io stato sì cortese mentre ch'io vissi is an archaic conditional perfect construction: 'Ben non sare' io stato' stands for modern 'Ben non sarei stato io' ('I would not have been'); sare' is the conditional auxiliary, stato the past participle 'been.' sì cortese = 'so/certainly courtly.' mentre ch'io vissi = 'while that I lived' (during my life) with vissi an archaic past form.
segments.purgatorio-11-g98: per lo gran disio de l'eccellenza ove mio core intese: per introduces cause/reason 'because of'; lo gran disio 'the great desire' + de l'eccellenza 'for excellence.' ove is a relative/adverb 'where/which' linking to 'excellence'; mio core 'my heart' is the logical subject of intese, which here (poetically) means 'felt/aspired to/was directed toward' (past tense).
segments.purgatorio-11-g99: Di tal superbia qui si paga il fio is a topical statement: Di tal superbia 'For such pride' (introducing the sin), qui 'here' (in this place of penitence), si paga is the impersonal/reflexive passive 'is paid' or 'one pays' (si + verb), and il fio 'the penalty/price' is the object being paid.
segments.purgatorio-11-g100: e ancor non sarei qui, is 'and still/yet I would not be here.' ancor (ancora) = 'yet/still'; non sarei is conditional 'I would not be'; qui 'here' indicates place (the speaker would not be in this state/place but for the reason that follows).
segments.purgatorio-11-g101: se non fosse che is a concessive conditional formula: se non fosse 'if it were not' followed by che introducing the explanatory clause; fosse is the imperfect subjunctive used in the protasis of a counterfactual conditional.
segments.purgatorio-11-g102: possendo peccar is a present gerund/absolute construction meaning 'being able to sin' or 'although I could have sinned.' possendo is the gerund of potere ('being able'), and peccar is the infinitive contraction of peccare 'to sin' used poetically.
segments.purgatorio-11-g103: mi volsi a Dio is a simple past action: mi volsi is the passato remoto (or perfect) of volgersi/voltare 'I turned (myself)'; the reflexive mi marks the subject turning; a Dio 'to God' is the direction of that turning (i.e., he repented or turned toward God).
segments.purgatorio-11-g126: La vostra nominanza is a noun phrase: the feminine definite article 'La' + possessive 'vostra' + the noun 'nominanza' (an abstract noun meaning fame/reputation). 'è' is the present indicative of essere linking the subject to the predicate noun phrase 'color d’erba' (a metaphor: lit. 'color of grass'). Note the poetic, compressed syntax: 'color d’erba' = 'the color of grass'.
segments.purgatorio-11-g127: 'che' is a relative pronoun referring back to 'color d'erba' ("which"). 'viene' and 'va' are present indicative forms of venire/andare respectively; the pair 'viene e va' is idiomatic and means 'comes and goes' (i.e. is fleeting). The simple present here describes a recurring or characteristic action.
segments.purgatorio-11-g128: The phrase begins with 'e' (and). 'quei' is a demonstrative pronoun ('that one' / 'he/it') referring to whatever agent discolors the grass's color. 'la' is a feminine object pronoun/article referring back to 'nominanza' (i.e. 'it'). 'discolora' is 3rd person singular present indicative meaning 'discolors' or 'fades'. 'per cui' is a relative phrase meaning 'by/through which'; 'cui' links to the following subordinate clause 'ella esce de la terra acerba' where 'ella' (feminine) again refers to the fame/colour. 'esce' is present indicative of uscire ('emerges/comes out'); 'de' is an archaic form of 'da' meaning 'from'; 'la terra acerba' = 'the bitter/sterile earth' (acerba describing the ground).
segments.purgatorio-11-g129: 'E io a lui' is simply 'And I (said) to him': 'E' = and, 'io' = I, 'a' = to, 'lui' = him. This is a short reporting clause; the verb of speaking is supplied by the following quoted clause.
segments.purgatorio-11-g130: Inside the quotation, 'Tuo vero dir' uses the infinitive 'dir' as a noun ('your true saying/word'). 'm’' is the clitic for 'mi' (me), attached to the verb 'incora' (an archaic/poetic form of 'incorare' here meaning to crown or honor); 'Tuo vero dir m’incora' = 'Your true word crowns me'. 'bona umiltà' is the object-complement 'good humility' (bona = good, umiltà = humility). Note: the dataset's senses for the clitic here did not match the pronoun function; interpretation as the reflexive/indirect object 'me' is correct.
segments.purgatorio-11-g131: 'e' = and. 'gran tumor' = 'great swelling' used metaphorically for 'great pride' (tumor = pride). 'm’' is again the clitic 'me/to me' attached to the verb. 'appiani' is a present-subjunctive/optative form of 'appianare' ('to level, to lay low'); here it expresses a wish or result meaning 'may it level/flatten my great pride' with 'Tuo vero dir' as understood subject. Poetically, the subjunctive expresses desire or hoped-for effect.
segments.purgatorio-11-g132: 'ma' = but; 'chi è quei' = 'who is that one' (quei acting as a demonstrative 'that one'); 'di cui' = 'of whom' (relative pronoun), 'tu parlavi' is the imperfect tense 'you were speaking/you spoke' (used to refer to the earlier speech), and 'ora' = 'just now' or 'a moment ago' modifying 'parlavi'. The clause is a direct question asking the identity of the person previously mentioned.
segments.purgatorio-11-g133: This segment is a punctuation mark (a period/full stop) signaling the end of the sentence. It has no lexical items, grammatical morphology, or syntactic role beyond terminating the sentence.
segments.purgatorio-11-g134: "Quelli è" is a demonstrative + verb phrase meaning 'that one is (he)'. The subject is expressed by the demonstrative 'Quelli' (an emphatic 'that one/those') while 'è' is the present indicative of essere acting as the copula.
segments.purgatorio-11-g135: Rispuose is the passato remoto (simple past) 3rd-person singular of rispondere, used narratively to mean 'he replied.' It stands alone as the reporting verb for the quoted words.
segments.purgatorio-11-g136: A proper-name sequence (given name + family name). The names are nominative/identifying and complete the quote: the speaker identifies the person.
segments.purgatorio-11-g137: This is a causal clause introduced by 'ed è qui perché' ('and he is here because ...'). 'Fu' is the passato (passato remoto/simple past) of essere, giving the reason in the past: 'he was presumptuous.' 'A recar' = a + infinitive (apocopated recar for recare) expresses purpose/action: 'to bring.' 'Tutta a le sue mani' = 'all into his hands' (metaphorical possession/control).
segments.purgatorio-11-g16: E come introduces a comparison ('and as'); the main clause is noi ... perdoniamo 'we forgive' (present indicative). lo mal is a neuter/abstract object 'the evil/harm' and ch' avem sofferto is a relative clause: avem is the archaic first-person-plural auxiliary 'have' (avemo/abbiamo) and sofferto is the past participle 'suffered.' Perdoniamo takes the indirect object a ciascuno 'to each one/everyone.'
segments.purgatorio-11-g17: E tu perdona is an address to 'you' and functions as an entreaty—perdona here reads as a second-person singular imperative or urgent present; benigno is a vocative adjective ('kind' or 'benign') addressing the addressee. The coordinating e links this clause to the previous one.
segments.purgatorio-11-g18: Non guardar is a negative command/entreaty 'do not look at/regard' (guardar = regard/look at). The object is lo nostro merto 'our merit/desert' (lo is the article/pronoun used poetically), so the clause asks the addressee not to take their merits into account.
segments.purgatorio-11-g19: Nostra virtù is the subject ('our virtue'). che introduces a relative clause modifying virtù. The phrase di legger s’adona contains an infinitive (legger, archaic form) introduced by di and the pronominal verb s'adona (si + adona) in the 3rd-person singular present; the reflexive clitic s’ refers back to virtù. The precise meaning of di legger is idiomatic/poetic (see translation note), and adona is an archaic/pronominal verb form (s’adona) meaning 'is disposed/apt' or 'adorns itself.'
segments.purgatorio-11-g20: Non spermentar is an infinitive with negative particle used as an injunction ('not to test' / 'should not be tested'); spermentar is an archaic infinitive ('to test, prove'). Con l’antico avversaro uses the preposition con 'with' plus the elided article l' and the adjective antico 'ancient' modifying avversaro 'adversary' (here a traditional epithet for Satan or the old enemy).
segments.purgatorio-11-g21: Ma libera is an imperative appeal addressed to the same 'you' as before: libera (from liberare) = 'free (it)'. Da lui 'from him' marks the agent/source to be removed, and che sì la sprona is a relative clause modifying lui ('who so spurs it'); la is the clitic direct-object pronoun referring back to virtù.
segments.purgatorio-11-g22: Quest' ultima preghiera is a noun phrase 'this last prayer'; quest' is the elided form of questa, ultima is the feminine adjective, and preghiera is the noun 'prayer.' It serves as the subject/topic of the following clauses.
segments.purgatorio-11-g23: Segnor caro is a direct vocative address to the addressee (segnor = signore, 'lord/sir'; caro = 'dear'). The comma indicates the apostrophe to the person being spoken to.
segments.purgatorio-11-g24: Già non si fa per noi uses si as the impersonal/passive clitic with fa (fare) to mean 'is made/is said' and già here means 'anymore' or 'no longer'; per noi 'for us' is the beneficiary. Together: 'is no longer said for us.'
segments.purgatorio-11-g25: Ché non bisogna is an explanatory clause: ché = 'for/because' and non bisogna is the impersonal present 'it is not necessary' (bisognare used impersonally). So it states the reason the prayer is no longer said for them.
segments.purgatorio-11-g26: Ma per color che dietro a noi restaro contrasts with the prior clause: ma = 'but'; per color che = 'for those who'; dietro a noi = 'behind us'; restaro is an archaic past-tense form (3rd plural) meaning 'they remained/were left behind.'
segments.purgatorio-11-g27: Così 'thus/so' sets the manner; a sé e noi 'for themselves and for us' marks the beneficiaries. buona ramogna 'good brushwood' is a metaphorical noun phrase (ramogna = small branches/fuel). Quell' ombre orando places the subject at the end in inversion: 'those shades, praying' (orando is a present participle 'praying' describing their action).
segments.purgatorio-11-g28: Andavan is the imperfect third-person plural of andare ('they were going' / 'they went'); sotto 'l pondo = sotto il pondo ('under the weight') — pondo is an archaic noun for 'weight' (or burden). The phrase describes their movement under a burden.
segments.purgatorio-11-g29: Simile a quel che talvolta si sogna is a simile: 'like that which one sometimes dreams.' Si sogna is the impersonal/reflexive construction 'one dreams' or 'is dreamed.' Talvolta = occasionally/sometimes.
segments.purgatorio-11-g30: Disparmente angosciate tutte a tondo: disparmente is an adverb 'differently/unequally'; angosciate is a past participle used adjectivally 'distressed/anguished'; tutte 'all'; a tondo is an idiomatic/archaic adverbial phrase meaning 'all around' or 'round about.' E lasse su per la prima cornice adds that they were 'and weary (lasse) upward (su) along (per) the first cornice' — cornice here names the first ring/ledge of Purgatory. Several forms are archaic or inverted for poetic effect.
segments.purgatorio-11-g31: Purgando is a present participle/gerund 'purging, cleansing' describing what the shades are doing; la caligine del mondo is the direct object 'the darkness/gloom of the world' (caligine = obscurity/murk), with del = of the. The gerund indicates simultaneous action or purpose.
segments.purgatorio-11-g104: An exclamatory noun phrase. "Oh" is an interjection; "vana" is an adjective (feminine) placed before the noun for emphasis meaning "vain/empty"; "gloria" is the head noun "glory/reputation." "de l’umane posse" is a prepositional phrase (de + definite article) meaning "of the human power/ability," where "umane" modifies "posse." The whole segment is an emotional reproach about the hollowness of human power.
segments.purgatorio-11-g105: Compact exclamatory clause. "Com'" is the contracted form of "come" here meaning "how"; "poco verde" is the noun phrase "little green" (i.e. little vegetation); "in su" is a locative pair meaning "upon/up on;" and "la cima dura" is the noun phrase "the hard summit/peak." Word order (adjective after noun omitted, preposition doubled "in su") is poetic but straightforward.
segments.purgatorio-11-g106: "Se non" here functions as a negative condition meaning "unless/if not." "È giunta" is the perfect (present of essere + past participle) meaning "has arrived/has come/has reached." "Da" with the following article marks the source/agent "by/from the..." and "l’etati grosse" (poetic plural of "età" with adjective "grosse") means "the long/weighty ages/years." Together the clause says that the little green appears only when long ages have reached the summit.
segments.purgatorio-11-g107: "Credette" is the passato remoto (simple past) third-person singular of "credere," meaning "he believed/thought." The subject "Cimabue" follows the verb (poetic inversion). "Ne la pittura" means "in painting" ("ne" = in/within), and the infinitive clause "tener lo campo" is an idiom meaning "to hold the field/to dominate the field/arena" — "tener" (infinitive) + direct object "lo campo."
segments.purgatorio-11-g108: "E ora" = "and now." "Ha Giotto il grido" is a neutral subject–verb–object order: "Giotto has the cry/outcry." Here "ha" is present indicative of "avere" and "il grido" figuratively means public acclaim or renown; the idiom "avere il grido" means to enjoy the popular shout of fame.
segments.purgatorio-11-g109: "Sì che" introduces a result clause meaning "so that/therefore." "La fama di colui" = "the fame of that one/that man" ("colui" points back to the previously named person), and "è scura" is present indicative "is dark/obscure," saying his reputation is now dimmed or eclipsed.
segments.purgatorio-11-g110: "Così" = "thus/so." "Ha tolto" is the present perfect (avere + past participle) meaning "has taken away/has deprived." The phrase "l’uno a l’altro" is a reciprocal/dative expression "from one to the other / from both of them" framing the transfer or deprivation, and the direct object is "la gloria de la lingua" = "the glory of the tongue/language" (i.e., poetic/literary renown). Word order places the affected parties before the direct object for emphasis.
segments.purgatorio-11-g111: "E forse" = "and perhaps." "È nato" (essere + past participle) means "has been born" or "is born" (present-perfect sense). "Chi" here is an indefinite relative/indefinite pronoun meaning "whoever/someone who," and "l’uno e l’altro" = "both the one and the other." "Caccerà" is the future tense of "caccare" meaning "will drive out/expel," and "del nido" = "from the nest" (del = di + il used as "from the" in this idiom). The clause predicts a new figure who will oust both predecessors.
segments.purgatorio-11-g160: Più non dirò places the adverb piú (more/any longer) before the negation + verb for emphasis; non negates dirò. Dirò is the future indicative, 1st person singular of dire, meaning “I will say.” The word order (adverb + non + verb) is poetic but straightforwardly means “I will say no more.”
segments.purgatorio-11-g161: e scuro so che parlo links two clauses; e = “and.” Scuro is an adjective used predicatively/adverbially to qualify the manner of speaking (“obscure/unclear”), placed before the verb for poetic effect. So is the present indicative 1st person of sapere (“I know”), and che introduces the subordinate clause parlo (present indicative 1st person of parlare, “I speak”).
segments.purgatorio-11-g162: ma poco tempo andrà begins a contrast: ma = “but.” Poco tempo is the noun phrase “little time,” and andrà is the future 3rd singular of andare used impersonally here — literally “little time will go,” idiomatically “it will not be long.”
segments.purgatorio-11-g163: che introduces a content clause (“that/when”) whose subject is i tuoi vicini (“your neighbors” — here written ’ tuoi). Faranno is future 3rd plural of fare and means “will make/bring about” in the periphrasis faranno sì che (“will act so that/ensure that”); sì che is a connective meaning “so that.” Tu potrai is future 2nd singular of potere (“you will be able”), and chiosarlo is the infinitive chiosare plus enclitic lo, “to annotate/comment on it.”
segments.purgatorio-11-g164: Quest’ is the contracted form of questa/questo (“this”) used before a noun; opera is the noun subject “work.” Li is an unstressed clitic pronoun (archaic/poetic placement) that functions as an object/clitic referring back to persons (here, “them”/“to them/from them”); tolse is passato remoto 3rd singular of togliere, “took away/removed”; quei confini = “those bounds/limits,” the direct object. The literal structure is “This work took those bounds from them.”
segments.purgatorio-11-g138: "Ito è così e va," uses the archaic adjective/participial form Ito (literally “gone”) as a predicate: 'Ito è' = 'he is gone'. The verbs that follow are in the present tense: 'è' (is) links to Ito, and 'va' (goes/proceeds) is simple present, joined by 'e' (and), so the clause says he is gone and continues to go on.
segments.purgatorio-11-g139: The phrase 'sanza riposo' is an archaic prepositional/adverbial phrase ('sanza' = modern senza) meaning 'without rest'; it modifies the preceding verb phrase (va).
segments.purgatorio-11-g140: 'poi che morì' is a temporal clause meaning 'after/since he died': 'poi che' functions like 'after' or 'since' and 'morì' is passato remoto (third person singular) 'he died'.
segments.purgatorio-11-g141: In 'cotal moneta rende a sodisfar chi è di là troppo oso' the noun phrase 'cotal moneta' ('such coin/money') is subject, 'rende' is present tense 'renders/returns/yields' and takes the structure 'rende a + infinitive' (here 'a sodisfar') meaning 'serves to/produces the effect of satisfying.' 'Chi è di là troppo oso' is a relative/indefinite clause 'who over there is too bold' ('di là' = 'over there/on that side', 'troppo oso' = 'too daring'). Note 'sodisfar' is an older infinitive form (modern soddisfare).
segments.purgatorio-11-g142: 'E io:' simply introduces the speaker ('And I'); the short clause marks Dante's interjection before his question.
segments.purgatorio-11-g143: ‘Se quello spirito ch’attende,’ begins a conditional clause: 'Se' = 'if', 'quello spirito' = 'that spirit', and 'ch’ attende' is a relative clause 'who waits' (attende = 3rd person present). Note: 'Se' here is the conditional conjunction 'if'.
segments.purgatorio-11-g144: 'pria che si penta' is a temporal subordinate clause: 'pria che' = 'before' and governs the verb 'si penta' ('he repents himself'); 'si' is the reflexive marking of 'pentare' and 'penta' is an older/subjunctive form meaning 'repent.'
segments.purgatorio-11-g145: 'l’orlo de la vita' is a noun phrase 'the edge/brink of life' (l’ = the, orlo = brink, de la = of the, vita = life); it specifies the temporal/liminal position of the spirit ('on the brink of life').
segments.purgatorio-11-g146: In 'qua giù dimora e qua sù non ascende' Dante contrasts locations: 'qua giù' = 'here below' and 'qua sù' = 'up here.' 'Dimora' is 3rd-person present 'dwells/stays' and 'non ascende' is a negated present 'does not ascend.' The word order places the downward location before the upward one for emphasis.
segments.purgatorio-11-g147: 'se buona orazïon lui non aita,' is another conditional: 'se' = 'if', 'buona orazione' = 'a good prayer', and the order 'lui non aita' places the object pronoun 'lui' (him) before the verb 'aita' (helps) in older syntax; 'aita' is the present 3rd-person of an older verb form meaning 'help'.
segments.purgatorio-11-g148: 'prima che passi tempo quanto visse' is a temporal clause: 'prima che' = 'before' (here taking the subjunctive 'passi' in meaning), 'tempo quanto visse' = 'the time equal to how long he lived' (literally 'time as much as he lived'), with 'visse' in passato remoto 'he lived.'
segments.purgatorio-11-g149: 'come fu la venuta lui largita?' is a passive/periphrastic question: 'come' = 'how', 'fu ... largita' = 'was ... granted' (essere + past participle largita), so literally 'how was his coming granted to him?' The pronoun ordering ('la venuta lui largita') is older/poetic: 'la venuta' (the coming) and 'lui' (to him).
segments.purgatorio-11-g150: A single punctuation mark (full stop) closing the speech line; no lexical items to analyze.
segments.purgatorio-11-g151: 'Quando' introduces a temporal clause ('when'). 'Vivea' is the imperfect indicative (3rd sing.) used to describe a past ongoing/state: 'he used to live' or 'he lived.' 'Più glorïoso' is a comparative phrase modifying the subject (understood 'he').
segments.purgatorio-11-g152: Disse is the passato remoto (3rd-person singular) of dire used as the narrative speech tag: 'he said.' It connects the reported speech to the narrator.
segments.purgatorio-11-g153: 'Liberamente' (adverb) modifies the omitted verb/action that follows; 'nel Campo di Siena' is a locative phrase ('in the Campo of Siena' — the public field/square). Together they set the scene where the action took place.
segments.purgatorio-11-g154: 'Ogne vergogna diposta' is a participial phrase: 'with every shame put aside.' 'Ogne' (every) modifies 'vergogna' (shame), and 'diposta' is a past participle ('disposed/laid aside') describing the state accompanying the subject.
segments.purgatorio-11-g155: S'affisse is reflexive (s' = si) + verb in the passato remoto form 'affisse'; here it means roughly 'he set himself' or 'he took his place/position' (an older/poetic reflexive verbal usage). It completes the preceding locative/attitudinal phrase ('he set himself there, shame aside').
segments.purgatorio-11-g156: Simple conjunction + locative: 'e lì' = 'and there,' linking the preceding action to the next clause.
segments.purgatorio-11-g157: 'Per' + infinitive ('trar', apocopated from trarre) expresses purpose: 'in order to draw/take (out).' 'L'amico suo' is the direct object ('his friend'), and 'di pena' indicates separation/from suffering ('out of suffering').
segments.purgatorio-11-g158: A relative clause introduced by 'ch’' (che → 'whom/that'): 'whom he was sustaining in the prison of Charles.' 'Sostenea' is imperfect indicative ('was sustaining/supporting'); 'ne la prigion di Carlo' = 'in the prison of Carlo/Charles.' Note: the short 'e' after 'ch’' is an archaic clitic for the subject 'he' (egli).
segments.purgatorio-11-g159: 'Si condusse a tremar' uses the reflexive 'si' with condusse (passato remoto of condurre) plus an infinitive 'tremar' (tremare, apocopated): literally 'he led himself to tremble' → 'he made himself tremble.' 'Per ogne vena' ('through every vein') is an emphatic bodily expression ('through every vein' = utterly/throughout his body).
segments.purgatorio-11-g112: Non è ... altro ch’ is a common negating formula meaning “is nothing other than.” The adjective mondan (worldly/secular) precedes romore (a poetic word for noise/rumour) for emphasis. Ch’ is the contracted form of che introducing the clause “that a …,” so un fiato di vento is the predicate (a breath/puff of wind).
segments.purgatorio-11-g113: Ch’or = che + or (ora) = “that now”; vien is present indicative (it comes). Quinci e quindi are an archaic pair meaning “from here and from there,” the repetition vien ... vien marking alternation.
segments.purgatorio-11-g114: E links the clause; muta here functions as a verb (mutare) meaning “changes” (3rd person singular present), nome = “name/reputation,” perché is causal “because,” and muta lato = “it changes side/direction.”
segments.purgatorio-11-g115: Che here is an interrogative meaning “what”; voce = “voice/fame”; avrai is the future tense of avere (“you will have”), with the subject tu placed after the verb for emphasis; più = “more.”
segments.purgatorio-11-g116: Se introduces a conditional “if”; vecchia is adjectival/participial “old” (elliptical with tu: “if you, old, …”); scindi is 2nd-person singular present of scindere “you tear away/separate”; da te la carne = “the flesh from you” (object after the preposition).
segments.purgatorio-11-g117: This is the comparative clause introduced by che (= “than”): se fossi morto is a contrary-to-fact subjunctive construction (fossi = imperfect subjunctive of essere) meaning “if you had been dead”; anzi che here means “before that”; lasciassi is the imperfect subjunctive of lasciare (“you had left/abandoned”) and refers to giving up the names/comforts ‘pappo’ and ‘dindi’ (nicknames; obscure terms).
segments.purgatorio-11-g118: Pria che = “before” (conjunction) governs a subjunctive clause; passin is a contracted/poetic form of passino (present subjunctive 3rd pl. of passare) “may pass”/“pass,” so the phrase means “before a thousand years pass.” Mill’ = mille (elision).
segments.purgatorio-11-g119: Ch’è is che è = “that is”; più corto spazio = “a shorter/briefer span” (spazio here = interval of time); a l’etterno = “to/for the Eternal” (a + definite article), so the phrase states a temporal comparison relative to eternity.
segments.purgatorio-11-g120: Ch’un muover di ciglia = “than a motion of an eyelash” (muover used as a noun = movement); al cerchio = “to the circle/sphere” (i.e. a heavenly sphere); che più tardi in cielo è torto is a relative clause: che = “which,” più tardi = “more slowly/most slowly,” in cielo = “in heaven,” and è torto (from torgere) = “is turned” or “turns” (poetic past participle), so the clause identifies the most slowly turning celestial sphere.
segments.purgatorio-11-g121: Colui che = “he who”; del cammin sì poco piglia = literally “takes so little of the path” (an idiom meaning he advances or participates only a little); dinanzi a me = “before/in front of me,” placing the person slightly ahead of the speaker. Piglia is present indicative 3rd singular.
segments.purgatorio-11-g122: Toscana sonò tutta uses sonò (passato remoto of sonare) with Toscana as subject: “all Tuscany resounded” or “all Tuscany rang out.” Tutta intensifies ‘all of Tuscany.’
segments.purgatorio-11-g123: E ora a pena = “and now, scarcely/with difficulty” (a pena = hardly); in Siena = “in Siena”; sen pispiglia is a colloquial/poetic expression meaning people there “prattle” or “mutter” about him — pispiglia (3rd person) = petty chatter, so the clause means he is now hardly mentioned in Siena.
segments.purgatorio-11-g124: Ond’ = ond’ (whence/from which) introduces the following information: era sire = imperfect “he was lord”; quando fu distrutta = “when it was destroyed” (fu distrutta = was destroyed, passato remoto); la rabbia fiorentina = “the Florentine rage/faction.”
segments.purgatorio-11-g125: Che introduces a relative clause describing that faction: superba fu = “was proud” (superba as predicate adjective with fu); a quel tempo = “at that time”; sì com’ ora è putta = “just as now it is putta,” where putta is a derogatory/pejorative adjective (poetic) meaning rotten/corrupt. The clause draws a direct comparison between past pride and present corruption.
segments.purgatorio-11-g32: Se introduces a conditional protasis ('if'). di là = 'on that side' or 'from there' (spatial adverbial). sempre ben per noi si dice is an impersonal/passive construction: si dice = 'it is said' or 'people say', with per noi indicating the persons concerned ('of/about us'); ben qualifies the verb ('speak well').
segments.purgatorio-11-g33: di qua = 'on this side' or 'from here'; che introduces a noun-clause ('what'); dire e far = the two infinitives 'to say and to do'; per lor = 'for them' (benefactive); si puote is an impersonal/implicative form meaning 'one can' / 'it is possible' (puote = can/has power). da quei = 'by those' (agent phrase) and c’ hanno al voler buona radice is a relative clause: 'who have a good root in their will' (hanno = have; al voler = 'to/for the will'; buona radice = 'good root' = good disposition or firm intention).
segments.purgatorio-11-g34: Ben si de' loro atar lavar le note is a purposive/assertive clause: Ben si (it is right/well that) + (impersonal) atar lavar (an infinitival sequence where lavar is the main action 'to wash/cleanse'); de' loro/lor(o) marks possession ('their') and atar (archaic/poetic) 'attached' or 'bound' modifies le note ('the marks/reputations/stains') that portar quinci ('which they bring from here'). The whole expresses obligation/wish that those marks be cleansed.
segments.purgatorio-11-g35: sì che introduces a result/purpose clause 'so that' connecting the previous obligation to the intended outcome.
segments.purgatorio-11-g36: mondi e lievi are two predicative adjectives describing the people after being cleansed: mondi = 'clean/purged' (here past-participial/adjectival sense), e lievi = 'and light' (physically or morally light/easy), forming the description for the following verb.
segments.purgatorio-11-g37: possano uscire is present subjunctive plural of potere + uscire: 'may be able to go forth/leave'; a le stellate ruote = 'to the starry wheels' (the celestial spheres), where a + definite article marks directionality toward the heavens.
segments.purgatorio-11-g38: Deh is an interjection (archaic) expressing entreaty or exclamation, like 'ah' or 'please'.
segments.purgatorio-11-g39: se introduces a conditional ('if'); giustizia e pietà are the compound subject ('justice and pity' / 'justice and mercy'); vi is the dative/indirect object 'to you'; disgrievi is an archaic verb form meaning 'offend/displease' (here used in the protasis with tosto 'soon/quickly'), so the clause reads 'if justice and pity soon offend you'.
segments.purgatorio-11-g40: sì che introduces a result/purpose clause ('so that'); possiate is a jussive/subjunctive form ('may you be able') giving the desired outcome; muover l’ala is an idiom 'to move the wing' = to take wing or begin to act/leave.
segments.purgatorio-11-g41: che introduces a relative/result clause modifying the previous 'so that' idea; secondo il disio vostro = 'according to your desire'; vi lievi is a subjunctive-form construction meaning 'may lift you' or 'may raise you' (lievi is an archaic/poetic form related to 'levare' or to the idea of being lighted up), so the whole reads 'that, according to your desire, may lift you.'
segments.purgatorio-11-g42: mostrate is the (imperative or indicative) verb 'show' (addressed to the listeners); da qual mano inver’ la scala = 'from which hand/side toward the ladder' (da qual mano = on which side; inver' = toward); si va più corto uses the impersonal si + andare: 'one goes' or 'the way goes' 'shorter' (più corto = shorter / less long).
segments.purgatorio-11-g43: e se c’è più d’un varco = 'and if there is more than one passage/opening' (c’è = ci è 'there is'; più d’un = 'more than one'); the clause sets up the alternative-case question about multiple ways.
segments.purgatorio-11-g44: quel ne ’nsegnate = 'show that one of them to us' (quel = that one; ne = 'of it/about it' or 'to us concerning it'; ’nsegnate = archaic form of 'insegnate' = 'indicate/show'); che men erto cala is a relative clause 'which descends less steeply' (men = less; erto = steep; cala = descends).
segments.purgatorio-11-g45: ché = 'for/because'; questi = 'this one' (referring to the companion); che vien meco = relative clause 'who comes with me' (vien = comes; meco = with me).
segments.purgatorio-11-g46: per lo ’ncarco de la carne d’Adamo = 'because of the burden/charge of Adam's flesh' (per = because of/through; lo 'ncarco = the burden/charge; de la carne d’Adamo = 'of the flesh of Adam'); onde si veste = 'with which he clothes himself' or 'in which he is clothed' (onde used as a relative 'with which/whereby' and si veste = 'he clothes himself').
segments.purgatorio-11-g47: al montar sù = 'upon climbing up' or 'when mounting' (al + infinitive expresses 'on/upon doing'): montar = to mount/ascend, sù = up.
segments.purgatorio-11-g48: contra sua voglia = 'against his will' (contra = against; sua = his; voglia = will/desire); this phrase qualifies the subject's unwillingness in the action just described.
segments.purgatorio-11-g49: è parco = 'is sparing/reluctant' (è = is; parco = sparing/stinted, here meaning 'meagre' or 'reluctant to expend effort' — applied to the companion's disposition while climbing).
segments.purgatorio-11-g50: Le lor parole is the noun phrase head: Le is the feminine plural article, lor is the unstressed possessive/pronominal adjective 'their' placed before the noun, and parole is a feminine plural noun meaning 'words/speeches.' The whole phrase functions as the topic (the thing being spoken about) that the following relative clause modifies.
segments.purgatorio-11-g51: This is a relative clause modifying 'parole.' che (relative 'which') introduces it; rendero is an archaic plural past form (rendero = renderono) meaning 'they returned/replied' or 'they gave back' and agrees with parole as its subject. a queste marks the indirect object 'to these (words/people),' and the nested clause che dette avea colui cu' io seguiva further qualifies those 'queste': dette is the feminine plural past participle 'said,' avea is the imperfect 'had' (auxiliary) marking the pluperfect 'had said,' colui is 'that man,' cu' (archaic for 'cui'/'che') = 'whom,' and io seguiva is an imperfect 'I was following.'
segments.purgatorio-11-g52: non fur is an archaic past tense (fur = furono/sono stati) meaning 'were not.' da cui is a prepositional relative phrase 'by/from whom' indicating agency or source; venisser is an archaic imperfect subjunctive (venissero) expressing potentiality ('might come' / 'might be made manifest'); manifeste is an adjective in feminine plural agreeing with parole meaning 'manifest/clear.' The clause says these words were not such that they could be clearly revealed by/unto anyone.
segments.purgatorio-11-g53: ma is the adversative conjunction 'but.' fu detto is a perfect/passive periphrasis (fu + detto) meaning 'it was said' or 'someone said' — an impersonal passive reporting speech that follows the previous description.
segments.purgatorio-11-g54: This begins direct speech. A man destra is an elliptical, idiomatic locution (short for a mano destra) meaning 'on the right hand/side'; per la riva means 'along the bank/shore' (per + definitive article + riva). con noi venite is an address to a plural 'you' — venite is the second-person-plural imperative or vocative present used here as a command/invitation 'come with us.'
segments.purgatorio-11-g55: e is 'and.' troverete is future indicative second-person-plural 'you will find.' il passo is the direct object 'the passage/step/path.' possibile modifies passo ('possible'). a salir is the prepositional infinitive construction 'to climb' (a + infinitive is common in older Italian), and persona viva is the noun phrase 'a living person' (viva agreeing in feminine with persona). The whole clause promises a passage that a living person can climb.

sentences.purgatorio-11-s17: And here it is necessary that I bear this burden for her, until God is satisfied, since I did not do it among the living, but here among the dead.
sentences.purgatorio-11-s18: Listening, I bowed my face downward; and one of them — not the one who was speaking — twisted himself under the weight that presses on them, and he saw me and recognized me and called, keeping his eyes with difficulty fixed on me, who went along entirely bowed with them.
sentences.purgatorio-11-s20: “I said to him, 'Are you not Oderisi, the honour of Gubbio and the honour of that art which is called illumination in Paris?'”
sentences.purgatorio-11-s13: And if I were not hindered by the rock that tames my proud neck, and therefore must keep my face bowed, I would look at these ones, who are still alive and have no name, to see whether I recognize him, and to make him pity this burden/body.
sentences.purgatorio-11-s14: I was Latin and born of a great Tuscan; Guiglielmo Aldobrandesco was my father; I do not know whether his name was ever among you.
sentences.purgatorio-11-s15: The ancient blood and the graceful deeds of my elders made me so arrogant that, not thinking of our common mother, I held every man in such contempt that I died of it — as the Sienese know, and every fellow in Campagnatico knows it.
sentences.purgatorio-11-s16: I am Omberto; and pride does not harm me alone, for she has drawn all my companions with her into misfortune.
sentences.purgatorio-11-s1: O our Father, who dwell in the heavens — not circumscribed, but by a greater love than that with which you have the first effects up there — may your name and your power be praised by every creature, as is fitting to give thanks to your sweet breath.
sentences.purgatorio-11-s2: May the peace of your kingdom come to us, for we cannot reach it by ourselves — if it does not come — even with all our effort.
sentences.purgatorio-11-s3: Just as your angels, by your will, offer sacrifice to you, singing "Hosanna", so may your men do likewise.
sentences.purgatorio-11-s4: Give us today our daily manna; without which, through this harsh desert whoever strives hardest to go forward goes backward.
sentences.purgatorio-11-s22: "Brother," he said, "the pages that Franco of Bologna paints shine more; the honor is all his now, and mine only in part."
sentences.purgatorio-11-s23: Indeed I would not have been so courtly while I lived, because of the great desire for excellence to which my heart aspired.
sentences.purgatorio-11-s24: For such pride the penalty is paid here; and yet I would not be here, if it were not that, having been able to sin, I turned to God.
sentences.purgatorio-11-s33: Your renown is the color of grass, which comes and goes, and which is discolored by that through which it comes out of the bitter earth.
sentences.purgatorio-11-s34: And I to him: "Your true word crowns me with good humility, and may it level my great pride; but who is that one of whom you were speaking just now?"
sentences.purgatorio-11-s36: “That is he,” he replied, “Provenzan Salvani; and he is here because he was presumptuous in bringing all Siena into his hands.”
sentences.purgatorio-11-s5: And as we forgive the harm we have suffered to every one, you too, kind sir, forgive and do not regard our merit.
sentences.purgatorio-11-s6: Our virtue, which is given to lightness, should not be tested with the ancient adversary, but free it from him who so urges it.
sentences.purgatorio-11-s7: This last prayer, dear lord, is no longer said for us — for it is not necessary — but for those who remained behind us.
sentences.purgatorio-11-s8: Thus those shades, praying, served as good brushwood for themselves and for us; they went under the burden, like what one sometimes dreams — each distressed differently, all round, and weary as they climbed up the first cornice, purging the world's darkness.
sentences.purgatorio-11-s25: O vain glory of human power!
sentences.purgatorio-11-s26: How little green there is upon the hard summit, unless it has been reached by the long ages!
sentences.purgatorio-11-s27: Cimabue thought he held the field in painting, and now Giotto has the acclaim, so that that man's fame is obscured.
sentences.purgatorio-11-s28: Thus Guido has taken the glory of language from both; and perhaps someone is born who will drive both of them from the nest.
sentences.purgatorio-11-s41: I will say no more — I know that I speak obscurely; but it will not be long before your neighbors will make it so that you can annotate it.
sentences.purgatorio-11-s42: This work took those bounds away from them.
sentences.purgatorio-11-s37: He is gone like that and goes on, without rest, since he died; such coin is paid to satisfy whoever over there is too bold.
sentences.purgatorio-11-s38: And I: "If that spirit who waits, before he repents, at the brink of life dwells here below and does not ascend up here; if a good prayer does not help him before the time equal to how long he lived has passed, how was his arrival to him granted?"
sentences.purgatorio-11-s40: “When he lived more gloriously,” he said, “freely in the Campo of Siena, with every shame laid aside he set himself there; and there, to draw his friend out of suffering — whom he was supporting in Charles's prison — he brought himself to tremble through every vein.”
sentences.purgatorio-11-s29: Worldly rumour is nothing but a puff of wind, now coming from here and now from there, and it changes its name because it changes side.
sentences.purgatorio-11-s30: What more fame will you have, if, when old, you strip the flesh from yourself, than if you had died before you gave up the ‘pappo’ and the ‘dindi’, before a thousand years pass?
sentences.purgatorio-11-s31: For there is a shorter span to the Eternal than the motion of an eyelash to the sphere which turns most slowly in Heaven.
sentences.purgatorio-11-s32: He who takes so little of the road before me made all Tuscany ring; and now in Siena he is hardly even whispered about, whence he was lord when the Florentine rage was crushed — which was then as proud as it is now rotten.
sentences.purgatorio-11-s9: If over there they always speak well of us, what can be said and done here for those who have a good root in the will?
sentences.purgatorio-11-s10: It is well that their attached marks which they bring from here be washed away, so that, cleansed and light, they may be able to emerge to the starry wheels.
sentences.purgatorio-11-s11: “Ah, if justice and pity soon offend you — so that you can move your wing, that it may lift you according to your desire — show from which side toward the ladder the way is shorter; and if there is more than one passage, point out to us that one which descends less steeply; for this one who comes with me, because of the burden of Adam's flesh with which he clothes himself, when climbing up is, against his will, sparing.”
sentences.purgatorio-11-s12: Their words, which were returned to those that that man whom I was following had said, were not such that they could be made manifest to anyone; but it was said: “Come with us on the right hand along the bank, and you will find a passage possible for a living person to climb.”
